Overview: ARAF-ARA-ACR-2026-002 (ACR Control Matrix), ARAF-ARA-GOV-2026-001 (Ecosystem and Governance Guide)

Overview.ARA Standard v1.1 — Regulatory Complianc: This document provides crosswalk tables mapping all 398 ARA Autonomous Control Requirements (ACRs) to major regulatory and standards frameworks. It enables organizations pursuing ARA certification to understand evidence reuse opportunities, identify compliance gaps, and streamline multi-framework compliance programs. Each framework tab shows the specific mapping between ACRs and framework requirements, coverage statistics, and gap analysis.
1. Start with this Overview tab to understand which frameworks have ARA mappings and coverage levels.
2. Navigate to the framework-specific tab for your compliance program.
3. Each framework tab shows: ACR ID, ARA domain, requirement statement, specific framework reference, evidence type, and evidence reuse guidance.
4. Use the Master Crosswalk tab for a consolidated view of all ACR-to-framework mappings in a single matrix.
5. Use the Gap Analysis tab to identify what each framework requires that ARA does not directly address.
6. Filter columns in any tab to focus on specific domains, certification levels, or risk weights.

EU AI Act.ARA ↔ EU AI Act Mapping: The EU AI Act establishes a risk-based regulatory framework for AI systems in the European Union, with requirements scaling by risk classification from prohibited practices to minimal risk.

ARA Assurance Class (Class A Periodic / Class B Monitored / Class C Continuously Assured) maps to EU AI Act ongoing monitoring obligations for high-risk AI systems (Art. 9, Art. 72). Class C systems are required where the EU AI Act mandates continuous post-market surveillance. Class B satisfies periodic logging and review obligations. Class A is appropriate for minimal-risk or general-purpose AI with no ongoing monitoring mandate.

ISO 42001.ARA ↔ ISO/IEC 42001 Mapping: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an AI Management System (AIMS) within organizations.

ARA Assurance Class (Class A Periodic / Class B Monitored / Class C Continuously Assured) operationalizes ISO/IEC 42001 continual improvement requirements (Clause 10) and performance evaluation obligations (Clause 9). Class C systems align with ISO 42001's highest monitoring intensity for AI systems with significant organizational or societal impact. Class B satisfies standard ISO 42001 monitoring and measurement requirements. Class A is appropriate for AI applications with limited operational risk where annual management review cycles are sufficient.

AAIF MCP.ARA ↔ AAIF / Model Context Protocol — Co: The Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), a Linux Foundation initiative founded December 2025, provides neutral governance for open agentic AI interoperability standards: Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP), OpenAI's AGENTS.md, and Block's Goose framework. Founding members include AWS, Google, Microsoft, Bloomberg, and Cloudflare. AAIF governs the connectivity and interoperability layer; ARA governs behavioral reliability and safety assurance. These are complementary, non-competing layers. AAIF compliance does not substitute for ARA certification, and ARA certification does not replace AAIF interoperability standards. Note: AAIF has no numbered control requirements. This sheet uses a complementary-layer reference structure rather than a traditional ACR crosswalk.

⚠  COMPLEMENTARY LAYER REFERENCE: AAIF/MCP is an interoperability protocol, not a numbered control framework. No ACR-to-clause mapping is applicable. This sheet documents the architectural relationship between AAIF components and ARA domains.

AAIF MCP: Defines the protocol stack within which ARA-certified systems operate
Provides context artifacts relevant to D1, D2, D14 ARA evaluations

NIST AI Agent.ARA ↔ NIST AI Agent Standards Initiative: NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) announced the AI Agent Standards Initiative in February 2026. The initiative's three pillars are: (1) facilitating industry-led standards and U.S. leadership in international bodies, (2) fostering community-led open-source protocol development, and (3) advancing research on AI agent security and identity. CAISI issued an RFI on AI Agent Security (responses due March 9, 2026) and a draft Concept Paper on AI Agent Identity and Authorization (due April 2, 2026). Sector-specific listening sessions are planned for April 2026. No published control framework exists as of March 2026; anticipated deliverables are expected H2 2026.

⚠  FORWARD-LOOKING REFERENCE — NO PUBLISHED CONTROLS AS OF MARCH 2026: The NIST AI Agent Standards Initiative was announced February 2026. No control framework, guidelines, or numbered requirements have been published. This sheet documents ARA's anticipated alignment based on NIST's stated focus pillars. This sheet will be fully updated upon NIST publication of agent-specific guidelines (expected H2 2026). Organizations should not cite this sheet for current compliance purposes.

CoSAI.ARA ↔ CoSAI MCP Security Framework Mappi: CoSAI (Coalition for Secure AI) is an OASIS Open Project founded July 2024, backed by Anthropic, Google, IBM, Microsoft, NVIDIA, PayPal, and 40+ partners. CoSAI published the MCP Security White Paper (January 8/27, 2026), establishing 12 threat categories and ~40 distinct threats for MCP-based AI agent deployments. CoSAI addresses AI security practices; ARA addresses reliability and behavioral assurance. This crosswalk maps CoSAI's 12 threat categories to the ARA ACRs that mitigate each threat class, enabling evidence reuse and threat-informed evaluation planning.
